questions.question: Explain Big-O notation and its significance in algorithm analysis. How does it differ from Big-Omega notation?
keyPoints: Big-O provides upper bound (worst-case)
Represents asymptotic upper limit
Used for worst-case analysis
Big-Omega provides lower bound (best-case)
Big-O: f(n) ≤ c·g(n), Big-Omega: f(n) ≥ c·g(n)

questions.question: Differentiate between O(n), O(n²), and O(log n) time complexities with one real-world example each.
keyPoints: O(1): Constant - accessing array element by index
O(log n): Logarithmic - binary search in sorted array
O(n): Linear - linear search in unsorted array
O(n²): Quadratic - nested loops or bubble sort
Growth rate comparison: log n < n < n²

questions.question: What is Theta (Θ) notation? When do we use Θ notation instead of O or Ω notation?
keyPoints: Θ provides tight bound (both upper and lower)
f(n) = Θ(g(n)) means f(n) is sandwiched between c1·g(n) and c2·g(n)
Used when upper and lower bounds are the same
Example: Linear search is Θ(n)
Better precision than O or Ω alone

questions.question: Explain the recurrence relation for Merge Sort: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n). Determine its time complexity using Master Theorem.
keyPoints: Merge Sort divides array into two halves
Solves two subproblems of size n/2 each
Merging takes O(n) time
Using Master Theorem: a=2, b=2, f(n)=n
n^(log₂2) = n, so f(n) = Θ(n), case 2: Θ(n log n)

questions.question: Compare and contrast O(2ⁿ) and O(n!) time complexities. Give one example algorithm for each.
keyPoints: Both are exponential time complexities
O(2ⁿ) grows as 2 to the power n
O(n!) grows as factorial of n
n! grows faster than 2ⁿ for n > 4
Example 2ⁿ: Tower of Hanoi, Fibonacci recursion
Example n!: Generating all permutations

questions.question: What is space complexity? How does it differ from time complexity? Discuss with an example.
keyPoints: Space complexity measures memory usage
Time complexity measures execution time
Space includes auxiliary space + input storage
Example: Merge Sort has O(n) space due to auxiliary array
Example: Quick Sort has O(log n) space for recursion stack

questions.question: Define Little-o and Little-omega notations. How are they different from Big-O and Big-Omega?
keyPoints: Little-o: strict upper bound (f(n) < g(n) asymptotically)
Little-omega: strict lower bound (f(n) > g(n) asymptotically)
Big-O/Ω can be tight or loose bounds
Little-o/ω are always non-tight bounds
lim f(n)/g(n) = 0 for o, ∞ for ω

questions.question: Discuss the basic efficiency classes in increasing order of complexity. Provide a brief description and one example algorithm for each class: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n²), O(n³), O(2ⁿ), O(n!).
keyPoints: O(1): Constant - array index access
O(log n): Logarithmic - binary search
O(n): Linear - linear search, traversal
O(n log n): Linearithmic - Merge Sort, Heap Sort
O(n²): Quadratic - Bubble Sort, nested loops
O(n³): Cubic - matrix multiplication (naive)
O(2ⁿ): Exponential - Tower of Hanoi
O(n!): Factorial - Permutation generation
Graph showing growth rates

questions.question: Explain the three asymptotic notations used for algorithm analysis: Big-O, Big-Omega, and Big-Theta. Provide mathematical definitions and illustrate with appropriate examples showing how f(n) = n² is bounded by each notation.
keyPoints: Big-O: f(n) ≤ c·g(n) for all n ≥ n₀ - Upper bound
Big-Omega: f(n) ≥ c·g(n) for all n ≥ n₀ - Lower bound
Big-Theta: c₁·g(n) ≤ f(n) ≤ c₂·g(n) - Tight bound
For f(n) = n²: f(n) = O(n³) or O(n²)
For f(n) = n²: f(n) = Ω(n) or Ω(n²)
For f(n) = n²: f(n) = Θ(n²)
Visual representation with curves

questions.question:
Analyze the time complexity of the following code fragments:
(a) for(i=0;i<n;i++)
 for(j=0;j<n;j++)
 print(i,j)
(b) for(i=1;i<n;i=i*2)
 print(i)
(c) for(i=0;i<n;i++)
 for(j=i;j<n;j++)
 print(i,j)
keyPoints: (a) Nested loops both 0 to n: O(n²) - prints n² times
(b) Doubles each iteration: runs log₂n times, so O(log n)
(c) Outer 0 to n, inner i to n: sum = n + (n-1) + ... + 1 = n(n+1)/2 = O(n²)
For (c): triangular number pattern
Time complexity comparison: (a) and (c) both O(n²) but (c) has fewer iterations
Best to worst: (b) < (c) < (a)

questions.question: What is the Master Theorem? Apply it to determine the time complexity of T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n². Also solve T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n using recurrence tree method.
keyPoints: Master Theorem: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)
Compare n^(logᵦa) with f(n)
Case 1: f(n) = O(n^(logᵦa-ε)) → Θ(n^(logᵦa))
Case 2: f(n) = Θ(n^(logᵦa) * logⁿ) → Θ(n^(logᵦa) * log n)
Case 3: f(n) = Ω(n^(logᵦa+ε)) → Θ(f(n))
For T(n)=3T(n/4)+n²: a=3, b=4, n^(log₄3)≈n^0.79, f(n)=n², Case 3: Θ(n²)
Recurrence tree for T(n)=2T(n/2)+n: levels log n, work n at each level, total Θ(n log n)
